messages.content: in 150 words at most please write me a professional, eloquent and gramatically impressive conclusion for the following question: "In your opinion, should students have unlimited access to these AI systems (e.g., Chat GPT, Claude 2, Bard etc.)? Please draw on personal examples as well as academic references to support your answer." Given that this is the answer I’ve conjured up for the question: “Technological innovation has thoroughly permeated countless facet of our existence, with the domain of education being no exception. In the midst of this exponentially evolving modernity of technological advancements, the dawn of generative artificial intelligence systems, have inaugurated a global debate regarding their integration and accessibility within education. Despite these avant-garde programmes, furnished with their prodigious yet arguably latent capacity to assimilate, generate, and customise content, bestowing upon students unparalleled opportunities for knowledge attainment and intellectual expansion; a cavalcade of potential pitfalls, ethical quandaries and pedagogical challenges that necessitate exhaustive academic scrutiny emerge. As we stand on the precipice of a new educational epoch, the widespread question emerges: Should students be granted boundless access to these systems? I contend that allowing students unfettered access to advanced AI tools could, contrary to expectations, impede educational progress and yield detrimental effects due to underlying ethical, scholastic, and developmental concerns. The quintessential contention in opposition to bestowing upon students unfettered access is firmly rooted in the principles aligned with academic integrity and the threat artificial intelligence constitutes to corroding any sense of it. Artificial intelligence systems, given their capabilities for content generation (contextually assignments/ essays etc.), could potentially facilitate an environment where original thought is negated yet supplanted by artificial intelligence generated responses, thereby calling into question the authenticity of ‘student- composed’ workings. (McCabe, et al., 2012) expound on the value of academic honesty as a cornerstone of educational institutions and learning processes whilst emphasising the principle that one’s work should be the product of one’s own understanding- an assertation of which I am wholly in accord with. Permitting students to the inexhaustible access of artificial intelligence programmes could undermine this tenet, as students may fabricate a grave dependence on generated material, thus impeding the cultivation of critical thinking and analytical abilities concurrently with invalidating any form of assessment process, which in theory is conceived to evaluate individual student learning and achievement (Newton, 2016). Such a dependency may involuntarily encourage a culture of lethargy and academic dishonesty (a culture of which I have personally bear witnessed where students would doubt and voluntarily omit their own academic facilities for insubstantial recapitulated artificially generated work), where plagiarism, not of texts, but ideas is conventional- subconsciously shifting the role of a student as an active learner to that of a passive regurgitator of generative artificial intelligence generated output; contributing to the devaluation of the educational process (Hughs & McCabe, 2006). Cognitive atrophy is a dormant threat in the scenario when enabling students to unlimited access to artificial intelligence generative programmes. Mechanisms of which may jeopardize and stifle the development of critical thinking and deep learning in students- two cornerstone objectives of education. Should students become overly dependent on artificial intelligence- this disproportionate dependence is also inversely proportional to compromising one’s prowess of maintaining prolonged concentration and introspective thought; there also lies a danger of fostering an ‘automation complacency’, a phenomenon whereby they (the students in question) subconsciously begin placing excessive trust in automated support, thereby attenuating their analytical rigor (Carr, 2011; Carr, 2014). To further accentuate this point, a study by (Sparrow, et al., 2011) found that people are less likely to retain information that is uncomplicatedly procurable online, suggesting that the ubiquitous nature of artificial intelligence could similarly affect memory and learning. Whilst being a computer science undergraduate at Cardiff Metropolitan University, we were tasked with devising and coding an optimised time complexity sorting algorithm. Delving into the intricacies of various sorting techniques, from bubble sort to selection sort to quicksort, this required substantial mental exertion and iteration. Though had we been given unrestricted access to the readily accessible assortment of modes of generative artificial intelligence, the looming temptation to simply generate the code would certainly have circumvented the valuable intellectual process of comparing efficiencies and leveraging algorithmic thinking. In accordance with (Bower & Sturman, 2015), educational technologies should be tools for the enhancement of learning, not substitutions for cognitive processes which in this example easily could have been the case. Data privacy/ security is another compelling argument against unbridled access to artificial intelligence systems. All platforms, whilst ostensibly altruistic in provisioning ‘free services’, operate within the data economy where user interactions are a commodity and gathered on an incomprehensible scale to tailor their outputs (Zuboff, 2019). Artificial intelligence platforms, necessitating an extensive trove of data in order to function optimally, not only raise concerns over data ownership and vulnerability but also emphasise that, as primary users, the student demographic inadvertently generates a considerable amount of ‘lucrative’ and sensitive personal data susceptible to commercial exploitation, security infringements, and identity theft (Solove, 2013; Zuboff, 2019). Rebuttals such as the following aforementioned render the notion of unconstrained access fundamentally at odds with, whilst simultaneously juxtaposing the ‘Privacy by Design’ (≅ data protection through technology design) principle (Cavoukian, 2009). This issue is further entangled by the nuances of informed consent; the more youthful student base may not possess the requisite depth of comprehending to providing informed consent pertaining to the use of their data whether it be for machine learning processes or not, in theory allowing artificial intelligence systems to capitalise on their naivety and erode personal autonomy in a realm where the sanctity of consent ought to be paramount (Roseenblat, et al., 2014). On the subject of data privacy/ security, I myself as someone who personally is averse to equipping students with unimpeded artificial intelligence access, reminisces on the reprehensible case of Facebook and the Cambridge Analytica scandals which servers as a stark reminder of the perils associated with vast data assemblages being mismanaged (Chadwalladr & Graham-Harrison, 2018).”

In conclusion, while generative AI systems offer a transformative potential for educational enrichment, my analysis advocates for moderated access rather than entirely limitless utilization by students. Nurturing originality, critical thought, and intellectual growth—core values underpinning academic integrity—are at stake when undue reliance on AI supplants independent learning. Empirical evidence and personal experience underscore the imperative to harmonize AI usage with fostering students' cognitive capabilities and safeguarding their data privacy. This pivotal balance prevents the downgrading of education to a superficial assimilation of prepackaged knowledge and circumvents the ethical missteps prevalent in an unchecked data economy. Incorporated responsibly, AI can undoubtedly be a dynamic ally in education; however, ensuring its role as an auxiliary tool rather than a crutch or a vulnerability necessitates conscious, measured policies. Therefore, rather than granting unlimited access, we must strive for a discerning integration of AI in education that empowers students without compromising the foundational elements of a robust learning experience.

please give me an array of revised sentences for the following that mantain professionalism, eloquence and are impressive gramatically: Overreliance on AI may inadvertently instil a culture of shortcut-seeking and surface learning, impairing students’ cognitive growth and diminishing their learning journey’s authenticity.

1. An undue dependence on AI risks fostering a tendency towards expediency and superficial comprehension, thereby stunting students' cognitive maturation and eroding the genuine quality of their educational odyssey.

2. The excessive use of AI can subtly cultivate a predilection for quick fixes and cursory knowledge acquisition, thereby undermining the intellectual development of students and devaluing the veracity of their academic pursuit.

3. Relying too heavily on artificial intelligence can subtly establish a convention of expedience over effort and cursory studies over in-depth learning, impeding students' intellectual evolution and detracting from the authenticity of their scholastic experiences.

4. Excessive reliance on AI platforms may unwittingly engender a milieu valorizing shortcuts and shallow scholarship, which can be detrimental to the cognitive progress of students and dilute the integrity of their learning trajectory.

5. Habitual dependency on AI technology has the potential to unwittingly inculcate a mindset inclined towards facilitation and elemental understanding, thereby hampering student's cognitive advancement and lessening the genuineness of their scholarly endeavors.

6. An overuse of AI tools might inadvertently promote a culture of seeking the path of least resistance and engaging in only surface-level learning, thus curtailing students' cognitive development and detracting from the ideal of an authentic educational experience.

7. Pervasive employment of AI within academic contexts can subtly breed a disposition towards seeking expedients and engaging in superficial study, thus impeding cognitive advancement and tainting the purity of the students' learning process.

8. The habit of leaning too heavily on AI threatens to subtly instigate a trend toward finding shortcuts and favoring a shallow grasp of subjects, impairing the cognitive elevation of students and tainting the authenticity of their learning endeavor.

9. Overindulgence in AI-based solutions may lead to the inadvertent establishment of a culture fixated on immediacy and minimal mastery, with deleterious effects on students' cognitive development and the sincerity of their journey in education.

10. An imprudent reliance on AI could insidiously foster a search for quick fixes and only a veneer of understanding, thereby compromising student cognitive progression and the true essence of their voyage through academia.

please give me an array of revised sentences for the following that mantain professionalism, eloquence and are impressive gramatically: Moreover, the unresolved issues of data privacy and cybersecurity loom large, with students—often unaware of the fine print—potentially becoming the unwitting currency in a data-centric economy

1. Furthermore, the looming concerns of data protection and cybersecurity present formidable challenges, as students, frequently oblivious to the subtleties of user agreements, risk becoming the inadvertent tender in an economy driven by information exchange.

2. Additionally, the pressing problems of data confidentiality and digital security cast a long shadow, where students—frequently uninformed about the nuances of consent—might unwittingly serve as the de facto tradeable asset within an economy fueled by data.

3. Moreover, the still-unaddressed issues of data sovereignty and information security are substantial, leaving students, often ignorant of the intricate terms of service, at risk of becoming the unintended bargaining chips in a data-dominated marketplace.

4. Besides, the persistent quandaries regarding data privacy and cyber protection remain significant, with students—typically unaware of the intricate details in contracts—potentially transforming into the innocent medium of exchange in a data-driven economic system.

5. In addition, the outstanding matters of personal data security and cyber safeguarding are critical, especially as students—commonly uninformed of the comprehensive agreements—may inadvertently be reduced to currency in a progressively data-reliant economy.

6. Similarly, the unresolved questions surrounding data privacy and cyber defenses remain acute, placing students—who are often ignorant of the elaborate specifics—into the precarious position of becoming the unintentional commodity in an increasingly data-focused economic landscape.

7. Additionally, the enduring concerns of data discretion and internet security command attention, with students—habitually unaware of the complex legal verbiage—potentially finding themselves the unknowing collateral in a market governed by data accumulation.

8. Moreover, the pervasive and still unresolved conundrums of data privacy and cybersecurity cast their ominous presence, with students—often blind to the granular details—risk being conscripted as the unintended currency within an economy that prizes data above all.

9. Also, the pressing conundrums of data protection and cyber resilience endure, ensnaring students—who are often naive to the exact terms and conditions—into potentially becoming the involuntary specie within the vast data marketplace.

10. Concomitantly, the intricate issues regarding the sanctity of personal data and the integrity of cybersecurity are paramount, with students—generally uninformed of the exhaustive fine print—risking becoming the unsuspicious monetary unit in an economy built on data traffic.
